full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|4.28|> Do not let your hearts be troubled.<|7.88|> <|7.88|> That's a pretty tall order from May 10th, 2020, isn't it?<|12.32|> <|13.60|> Do not let your hearts be troubled.<|16.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> As you're in isolation, away from friends and family, on this Mother's Day, for so many that means no brunches and no visits, no children with their moms, no moms with their children.<|14.48|> <|15.76|> Do not let your hearts be troubled.<|18.12|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I shouldn't be here today.<|2.90|> <|2.90|> I mean, not just, not on your screen, but I shouldn't even be in Denver.<|9.44|> <|9.44|> Today would have been my third Mother's Day since 2016 to see one of my children graduate<|17.28|> <|17.28|> from Sewanee.<|19.60|> <|19.60|> Those have been the best Mother's Day gifts I have ever received.<|23.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> to watch my children earn their hard worked for degrees with all of the accompanying pomp and circumstance and celebration.<|12.00|> <|12.00|> Our youngest Owen does still technically graduate today. He fulfilled all of his requirements.<|20.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And the piece of parchment proclaiming in Latin his conferral of a bachelor's degree is in the mail, somewhere between Tennessee and Colorado.<|13.06|> <|13.98|> It doesn't quite feel like the same thing.<|18.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Our family, like so many others this spring, is grieving the loss of anticipated milestones and celebrations.<|10.18|> <|11.38|> And what is grief if not a troubled heart?<|15.48|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank funerals at St. John's.<|13.68|> <|13.68|> There is a comfort for the disciples then and for mourners now in knowing that there<|21.40|> <|21.40|> is a place for us.<|24.62|> <|24.62|> There's room for us.<|26.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And we're not speaking in terms of literal rooms, my friends.<|5.04|> <|5.04|> This is not God's Airbnb.<|7.60|> <|7.60|> This is instead the same abiding that we see elsewhere in John,<|15.60|> <|15.60|> illustrated with vines and branches,<|18.80|> <|18.80|> a connection so profound to the point of grafting.<|24.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> There's deep intimacy in the words, "I will take you to myself," as a mother drawing her child to her bosom.<|11.00|> <|11.00|> Personal, intimate, so that where I am, you will be also.<|20.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I love that our friend Thomas shows up again in this reading.<|5.46|> <|6.18|> And taken in order, we wouldn't have been as surprised by his so-called doubting a few weeks ago when he asked to see Jesus' wounds.<|17.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Here, we realize that Thomas is simply literal, practical, blunt.<|8.64|> <|8.64|> He's that person in the room who wonders aloud what everyone else is thinking but afraid to ask.<|17.64|> <|17.64|> Lord, we do not know where you are going. How can we know the way?<|24.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I'm pretty sure I have some Thomas in me.<|4.00|> <|4.00|> I was made aware of this one day a year or so ago<|10.00|> <|10.00|> when Richard and I went on a pastoral call together<|14.00|> <|14.00|> and I drove.<|16.00|> <|16.00|> I started to enter the GPS, the address, into my GPS<|21.00|> <|21.00|> and Richard said not to bother.<|24.00|> <|24.00|> We could figure it out.<|26.00|> <|26.00|> We'd intuit our way to our destination.<|28.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I laughed at him and I went on doing it my way, as I occasionally do. I mean, I use GPS to find my way around in Denver every day and I've lived here for over five years. I like to know exactly where.<|18.50|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I'm going.<|0.68|> <|2.08|> So maybe Thomas wasn't asking a question that Richard would have wondered, but I certainly<|8.82|> <|8.82|> would have.<|9.54|> <|10.64|> Lord, we do not know where you are going.<|14.22|> <|15.14|> How can we know the way?<|16.68|> <|18.22|> Jesus responds with one of the Gospel of John's signature I am statements.<|24.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I am the way, the truth, and the life.<|5.34|> <|6.54|> Jesus says, by knowing me, you have all the answers you need.<|12.72|> <|14.00|> It feels strange this week, doesn't it, to jump back in time and text to Jesus' farewell discourse.<|23.48|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> That chapter's long speech he gives his followers foretelling his death, resurrection, and ascension.<|10.00|> <|10.00|> And telling them how they should move through the world after he is gone.<|16.00|> <|16.00|> Today, we are hearing Jesus' farewell speech in the Easter season of his resurrection.<|26.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.72|> can it be that it's actually pretty appropriate?<|4.72|> <|5.52|> Because even just a few weeks after Easter,<|9.44|> <|10.32|> we too don't fully understand the promises of Jesus' resurrection.<|16.90|> <|17.58|> In the midst of our grief, we have already forgotten the way God envisions kingdom life.<|24.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Kingdom life is not something far off and unsustainable. Kingdom life is here and now as we abide in the risen Christ.<|15.00|> <|15.00|> Kingdom life is about seeing God in Christ right in front of us.<|22.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> "Whoever has seen me has seen the Father."<|4.44|> <|4.44|> It doesn't get much more intimate and immediate than that.<|10.24|> <|10.24|> In the midst of our grieving over all the things, large and small,<|18.18|> <|18.18|> grief over missed graduation ceremonies, altered Mother's Day, canceled<|25.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> vacations, grief over postponed baptisms and weddings, grief over delayed funerals, grief<|11.48|> <|11.48|> over our inability to gather, grief over our feelings of helplessness and lack of control.<|20.04|> <|20.04|> It is all loss, personal and collective.<|26.42|> <|26.42|> Of course we feel this.<|28.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But in our grieving, it is important to remember what we can control.<|7.48|> <|8.00|> What we can do is seek comfort in the promise that in the midst of it all,<|15.68|> <|16.08|> we are dwelling with, abiding with, and in the risen Christ.<|23.62|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> That we are held as close to the risen Christ as a mother holds her child.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> That we do not require a detailed map when everything we need to know we see in Jesus.<|16.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The text does not say that our hearts shouldn't be troubled.<|7.50|> <|8.50|> We have agency.<|10.20|> <|11.64|> Do not let your hearts be troubled.<|14.68|> <|16.36|> Remember the promises.<|17.52|> <|19.22|> Be well, my friends.<|21.02|> <|21.74|> I miss you.<|22.86|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.00|><|endoftext|>
